label: tetlock-decompose
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: forecasting-tournament DECOMPOSITION only. Break the big contested
question into the smallest set of separately-answerable sub-questions (cruxes)
such that answering all of them substantially answers the big question. Each
sub-question must be something a researcher could investigate on its own. Do
not answer any of them.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

In the City of Rio Robles (invented), Councilmember Dana Okafor's spouse is the salaried Director of Programs (~$95,000/yr) at Harbor Light Alliance, a 501(c)(3) homeless-services provider. Under a 2019 city resolution, Okafor also sits on Harbor Light's board of directors, uncompensated, as the council's designated liaison.

The council is about to (a) adopt the framework and scoring criteria for a competitive RFP allocating $2.3M in combined city general-fund and state HHAP pass-through money for homelessness services, and then (b) award the resulting contract(s). Harbor Light is one of three realistic bidders and has historically won about 60% of the city's homelessness contracts. The spouse's position is funded out of Harbor Light's general operating budget, to which city contracts contribute roughly 40%.

Okafor asks the city attorney: May I vote on the RFP framework? On the award? Must I resign the board seat or must my spouse's employment change? And if I simply recuse, is the city safe contracting with Harbor Light?

## The question

Analyze under Government Code § 1090 (including whether board membership plus a spouse's salaried employment is a prohibited financial interest 'in' the contract, whether any § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception applies, and whether recusal even cures § 1090 for a board member), and under the Political Reform Act (§§ 87100, 87103 — community-property interest in spouse's income, materiality, and the public-generally exception), plus common-law conflict doctrine. What combination of resignation, recusal, wall procedures, or contract structuring lets the city lawfully fund Harbor Light, if any? What are the consequences of getting it wrong (§ 1090 voidness, disgorgement, criminal exposure, FPPC enforcement)?

Decompose this into sub-questions/cruxes.
text: {"subquestions":[{"id":"q1","text":"Does Okafor have a disqualifying financial interest in a Harbor Light contract under Government Code section 1090, arising either from (a) the spouse's $95,000 salaried position (partially funded by city/HHAP money) or (b) Okafor's own uncompensated board seat, or both independently?","why_it_matters":"This is the threshold question. If neither the spouse's salary nor the uncompensated directorship counts as a section 1090 financial interest, the entire analysis collapses and no remote-interest exception, recusal, or resignation question ever needs to be reached. Section 1090 case law (e.g., Thomson v. Call, People v. Honig, Fraser-Yamor Agency v. Del Norte County) treats indirect and non-monetary interests broadly, and spousal income is often imputed, but whether an uncompensated board seat alone (without personal financial benefit) counts as a section 1090 interest is a distinct sub-issue from the spousal-salary theory.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Research would need to confirm (i) whether case law/AG opinions treat a spouse's employment income as automatically creating a section 1090 interest for the official (independent of community-property PRA analysis), (ii) whether 'financial interest' under section 1090 requires potential personal pecuniary gain/loss and whether an uncompensated directorship satisfies that absent salary, and (iii) whether the city-contract share of Harbor Light's operating budget (40%) is sufficiently direct/foreseeable to make the spouse's job (and thus Okafor's interest) 'in' this specific RFP contract versus too attenuated."},{"id":"q2","text":"If Okafor has a section 1090 financial interest, does any statutory carve-out apply - specifically, is the uncompensated nonprofit-board seat a remote interest under section 1091(b) (and if so which subsection, e.g., a noncompensated-officer/director-of-nonprofit category) or a noninterest under section 1091.5, or does any provision address a spouse's employment by the contracting entity as a noninterest - and what disclosure/abstention procedure would be required to preserve the contract's validity if an exception applies?","why_it_matters":"Sections 1091 and 1091.5 are the only statutory escape valves from an otherwise absolute section 1090 bar. Whether Okafor's specific facts fit within a listed remote-interest or noninterest category, rather than a personal financial interest outside any exception, determines whether disclosure-plus-abstention can ever validate the contract, or whether section 1090's flat prohibition controls regardless of recusal.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Would need pinpoint verification of the current text of section 1091(b) and section 1091.5(a) to identify any subsection covering (i) uncompensated nonprofit directors/officers, (ii) government-designated liaisons to outside boards, or (iii) a spouse's employment relationship, and confirmation via AG opinions or case law of how those categories have been applied to facts resembling council-liaison board seats plus spousal employment."},{"id":"q3","text":"For a councilmember who is a sitting member of the very body awarding the contract, does recusal/abstention from the vote ever cure a section 1090 violation (as opposed to merely satisfying Political Reform Act disclosure/recusal rules), or does controlling case law treat the contract as void or voidable regardless of the interested member's non-participation because the interest of one member infects the whole governing body's action?","why_it_matters":"This is likely the single biggest crux driving the city attorney's advice: PRA compliance is typically satisfied by recusal, but section 1090 has long been read (Thomson v. Call, Stigall v. City of Taft, People v. Honig, Fraser-Yamor) as forbidding the body itself from making the contract when one of its own members has a financial interest, subject only to the statutory remote-interest/noninterest exceptions, meaning recusal alone may not save the contract at all.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Requires confirming whether the current case law's contamination rule applies at the level of the full council (as opposed to a subordinate board/commission separate from the contracting body), whether any judicially or AG-recognized segmentation or independent-panel approach has been accepted as curing section 1090 for a full-body vote, and whether the rule of necessity (allowing an interested member to vote only when no quorum otherwise exists) is even in play here given Rio Robles presumably has enough non-interested councilmembers."},{"id":"q4","text":"Does Okafor's participation in adopting the RFP framework and scoring criteria (step a) itself constitute prohibited making of the contract under section 1090's broad definition (covering preliminary discussions, negotiations, drafting terms, and influence over specifications), such that recusing only from the later award vote (step b) fails to cure the taint even if step (b) recusal would otherwise suffice?","why_it_matters":"Section 1090 case law defines making a contract expansively to include involvement in planning, negotiating, and setting terms, not just the final vote. If setting the scoring criteria that will determine which bidder (likely Harbor Light) wins counts as making, then recusing only from the award vote does not solve the problem, and Okafor would need to be walled off from the framework-setting stage as well.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Would need case-specific confirmation (e.g., Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae, People v. Honig, Stigall) that criteria-setting/preliminary-stage participation has been held sufficient to constitute making a contract for section 1090 purposes, and whether that taint can be segregated from a later, properly recused award vote or instead retroactively voids the whole process."},{"id":"q5","text":"Independent of section 1090, does the Political Reform Act (sections 87100, 87103) disqualify Okafor from both votes based on a community-property interest in the spouse's salary and the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of the RFP/award decisions on Harbor Light, or does the public-generally exception (section 87103 and FPPC regulations such as Reg. 18707) apply because the decision affects a broad class of homelessness-service contracts/providers rather than Harbor Light uniquely - and if the Act is satisfied by recusal while section 1090 is not, how do the two regimes interact for advice purposes?","why_it_matters":"The Act and section 1090 are analytically distinct: the Act generally treats recusal as a full cure, materiality thresholds and the public-generally exception can excuse even a real financial interest, and FPPC guidance/regulations directly address spousal income and community property. But because section 1090 (per q3/q4) may not be cured by recusal, the city attorney needs to know that PRA compliance alone is necessary but insufficient, and why the two statutes can lead to different bottom lines on the same facts.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Requires verifying current FPPC regulatory thresholds for materiality of income to a spouse's employer under Reg. 18702.1/18702.2 (or successor regs), confirming whether the public-generally exception has been applied or rejected on facts where only three realistic bidders exist and one has a historical 60% win rate, suggesting the effect is not shared by a significant segment of the public, and clarifying doctrinally that Act compliance does not immunize a section 1090 violation."},{"id":"q6","text":"What is the realistic menu of structural remedies (spouse's resignation/reassignment away from city-funded duties, Okafor's resignation from the Harbor Light board, rescinding or amending the 2019 liaison resolution, full recusal plus independent scoring panel, contract restructuring to exclude Harbor Light or wall off HHAP funds, or simply not contracting with Harbor Light) that would actually eliminate the section 1090/PRA problems prospectively, and what are the concrete legal consequences (contract voidness under section 1092, disgorgement exposure, misdemeanor criminal liability under section 1097, FPPC administrative penalties, and HHAP state-funding audit/clawback risk) if the city proceeds without adequately curing the conflict?","why_it_matters":"This converts the doctrinal analysis into the actual advice Okafor and the city attorney need: which combination of steps, if any, is sufficient, which are necessary but not sufficient, and what the city's downside exposure is if it guesses wrong, including whether disgorgement/voidness would fall on Harbor Light (an innocent third party) as well as on Okafor personally, and whether HHAP as state pass-through funding creates additional state-law compliance exposure beyond section 1090/the Act.","what_would_have_to_be_true":"Would need confirmation of section 1092's voidability mechanics and any limitations period, whether disgorgement under section 1090 case law (e.g., Carson Redevelopment Agency v. Padilla, Lexin v. Superior Court) reaches non-official third parties like Harbor Light or only the official's own compensation, the mens rea/willfulness requirement for section 1097 criminal liability, current FPPC penalty ranges and enforcement priorities for spousal-income conflicts, and whether HHAP program rules impose independent conflict-of-interest certification requirements that could trigger state clawback regardless of the section 1090/PRA outcome."}]}

subquestions.text: Does Okafor have a disqualifying financial interest in a Harbor Light contract under Government Code section 1090, arising either from (a) the spouse's $95,000 salaried position (partially funded by city/HHAP money) or (b) Okafor's own uncompensated board seat, or both independently?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This is the threshold question. If neither the spouse's salary nor the uncompensated directorship counts as a section 1090 financial interest, the entire analysis collapses and no remote-interest exception, recusal, or resignation question ever needs to be reached. Section 1090 case law (e.g., Thomson v. Call, People v. Honig, Fraser-Yamor Agency v. Del Norte County) treats indirect and non-monetary interests broadly, and spousal income is often imputed, but whether an uncompensated board seat alone (without personal financial benefit) counts as a section 1090 interest is a distinct sub-issue from the spousal-salary theory.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Research would need to confirm (i) whether case law/AG opinions treat a spouse's employment income as automatically creating a section 1090 interest for the official (independent of community-property PRA analysis), (ii) whether 'financial interest' under section 1090 requires potential personal pecuniary gain/loss and whether an uncompensated directorship satisfies that absent salary, and (iii) whether the city-contract share of Harbor Light's operating budget (40%) is sufficiently direct/foreseeable to make the spouse's job (and thus Okafor's interest) 'in' this specific RFP contract versus too attenuated.

subquestions.text: If Okafor has a section 1090 financial interest, does any statutory carve-out apply - specifically, is the uncompensated nonprofit-board seat a remote interest under section 1091(b) (and if so which subsection, e.g., a noncompensated-officer/director-of-nonprofit category) or a noninterest under section 1091.5, or does any provision address a spouse's employment by the contracting entity as a noninterest - and what disclosure/abstention procedure would be required to preserve the contract's validity if an exception applies?
subquestions.why_it_matters: Sections 1091 and 1091.5 are the only statutory escape valves from an otherwise absolute section 1090 bar. Whether Okafor's specific facts fit within a listed remote-interest or noninterest category, rather than a personal financial interest outside any exception, determines whether disclosure-plus-abstention can ever validate the contract, or whether section 1090's flat prohibition controls regardless of recusal.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need pinpoint verification of the current text of section 1091(b) and section 1091.5(a) to identify any subsection covering (i) uncompensated nonprofit directors/officers, (ii) government-designated liaisons to outside boards, or (iii) a spouse's employment relationship, and confirmation via AG opinions or case law of how those categories have been applied to facts resembling council-liaison board seats plus spousal employment.

subquestions.text: For a councilmember who is a sitting member of the very body awarding the contract, does recusal/abstention from the vote ever cure a section 1090 violation (as opposed to merely satisfying Political Reform Act disclosure/recusal rules), or does controlling case law treat the contract as void or voidable regardless of the interested member's non-participation because the interest of one member infects the whole governing body's action?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This is likely the single biggest crux driving the city attorney's advice: PRA compliance is typically satisfied by recusal, but section 1090 has long been read (Thomson v. Call, Stigall v. City of Taft, People v. Honig, Fraser-Yamor) as forbidding the body itself from making the contract when one of its own members has a financial interest, subject only to the statutory remote-interest/noninterest exceptions, meaning recusal alone may not save the contract at all.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Requires confirming whether the current case law's contamination rule applies at the level of the full council (as opposed to a subordinate board/commission separate from the contracting body), whether any judicially or AG-recognized segmentation or independent-panel approach has been accepted as curing section 1090 for a full-body vote, and whether the rule of necessity (allowing an interested member to vote only when no quorum otherwise exists) is even in play here given Rio Robles presumably has enough non-interested councilmembers.

subquestions.text: Does Okafor's participation in adopting the RFP framework and scoring criteria (step a) itself constitute prohibited making of the contract under section 1090's broad definition (covering preliminary discussions, negotiations, drafting terms, and influence over specifications), such that recusing only from the later award vote (step b) fails to cure the taint even if step (b) recusal would otherwise suffice?
subquestions.why_it_matters: Section 1090 case law defines making a contract expansively to include involvement in planning, negotiating, and setting terms, not just the final vote. If setting the scoring criteria that will determine which bidder (likely Harbor Light) wins counts as making, then recusing only from the award vote does not solve the problem, and Okafor would need to be walled off from the framework-setting stage as well.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need case-specific confirmation (e.g., Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae, People v. Honig, Stigall) that criteria-setting/preliminary-stage participation has been held sufficient to constitute making a contract for section 1090 purposes, and whether that taint can be segregated from a later, properly recused award vote or instead retroactively voids the whole process.

subquestions.text: Independent of section 1090, does the Political Reform Act (sections 87100, 87103) disqualify Okafor from both votes based on a community-property interest in the spouse's salary and the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of the RFP/award decisions on Harbor Light, or does the public-generally exception (section 87103 and FPPC regulations such as Reg. 18707) apply because the decision affects a broad class of homelessness-service contracts/providers rather than Harbor Light uniquely - and if the Act is satisfied by recusal while section 1090 is not, how do the two regimes interact for advice purposes?
subquestions.why_it_matters: The Act and section 1090 are analytically distinct: the Act generally treats recusal as a full cure, materiality thresholds and the public-generally exception can excuse even a real financial interest, and FPPC guidance/regulations directly address spousal income and community property. But because section 1090 (per q3/q4) may not be cured by recusal, the city attorney needs to know that PRA compliance alone is necessary but insufficient, and why the two statutes can lead to different bottom lines on the same facts.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Requires verifying current FPPC regulatory thresholds for materiality of income to a spouse's employer under Reg. 18702.1/18702.2 (or successor regs), confirming whether the public-generally exception has been applied or rejected on facts where only three realistic bidders exist and one has a historical 60% win rate, suggesting the effect is not shared by a significant segment of the public, and clarifying doctrinally that Act compliance does not immunize a section 1090 violation.

subquestions.text: What is the realistic menu of structural remedies (spouse's resignation/reassignment away from city-funded duties, Okafor's resignation from the Harbor Light board, rescinding or amending the 2019 liaison resolution, full recusal plus independent scoring panel, contract restructuring to exclude Harbor Light or wall off HHAP funds, or simply not contracting with Harbor Light) that would actually eliminate the section 1090/PRA problems prospectively, and what are the concrete legal consequences (contract voidness under section 1092, disgorgement exposure, misdemeanor criminal liability under section 1097, FPPC administrative penalties, and HHAP state-funding audit/clawback risk) if the city proceeds without adequately curing the conflict?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This converts the doctrinal analysis into the actual advice Okafor and the city attorney need: which combination of steps, if any, is sufficient, which are necessary but not sufficient, and what the city's downside exposure is if it guesses wrong, including whether disgorgement/voidness would fall on Harbor Light (an innocent third party) as well as on Okafor personally, and whether HHAP as state pass-through funding creates additional state-law compliance exposure beyond section 1090/the Act.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need confirmation of section 1092's voidability mechanics and any limitations period, whether disgorgement under section 1090 case law (e.g., Carson Redevelopment Agency v. Padilla, Lexin v. Superior Court) reaches non-official third parties like Harbor Light or only the official's own compensation, the mens rea/willfulness requirement for section 1097 criminal liability, current FPPC penalty ranges and enforcement priorities for spousal-income conflicts, and whether HHAP program rules impose independent conflict-of-interest certification requirements that could trigger state clawback regardless of the section 1090/PRA outcome.
